topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The acceptance letter sat on the kitchen counter, crisp and mocking. Leo picked it up, the glossy paper crinkling under his thumb. He reread the line: "We regret to inform you that your application was not selected for the varsity squad." A low, frustrated sound escaped his throat, a puff of air that seemed too loud in the quiet apartment. He slammed the letter down, the sound echoing sharply off the linoleum floor. He walked over to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, his jaw tight. He ran a hand through his already messy hair, the gesture sharp and jerky. Why? He’d trained harder than anyone, sacrificed sleep, pushed through the nagging injuries. Now, this. He felt a hot flush creep up his neck, a tight knot forming right behind his sternum. He needed to move, to do something that wasn't this hollow, sinking feeling.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Marcus muttered, the word tasting like ash. He paced the sterile locker room, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead amplifying the sound of his heavy footsteps. He stopped abruptly in front of his reflection in the mirrored wall. His face was set in a grim mask, the muscles in his neck visibly tense. He scrutinized the physique he’d painstakingly built, the calluses on his hands, the sheer volume of hours poured into this pursuit. It all felt pointless, a monumental waste of energy. He clenched his fists until his knuckles were white, the tendons standing out like cords under the skin. He let out a slow, controlled breath, trying to regulate the sudden surge of heat rising in his chest. He turned away from the mirrors, focusing instead on the scuff marks on the floor, his gaze fixed with an intense, almost angry focus.

[story 3]

"Another rejection." Sarah sighed, the sound heavy and defeated. She slumped onto the edge of the worn sofa, the fabric groaning under her weight. She picked up her phone, the screen illuminating the disappointment etched across her features. Every email, every practice session, every early morning run seemed to culminate in this single, crushing outcome. She scrolled through the team roster again, her eyes moving over the names of those who had gotten the spot, the ones who seemed effortlessly destined for success. A tremor ran through her hands as she set the phone down on the coffee table, the movement stiff and jerky. She stood up, walking to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to let in a rush of cold, indifferent air. She didn't bother to look at the view; she just stood there, breathing in deeply, trying to physically push the heavy weight in her chest away.
